includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)

items.title: Consensus is unanimous on a soft June CPI — that's exactly the setup Paper Alfa distrusts
items.idea: Heading into Tuesday's CPI print, even forecasters with the strongest track records are calling for a core reading softer than Bloomberg consensus, helped by residual seasonality, falling gasoline and favorable base effects. The author's actual thesis isn't that the number will miss — it's that markets become most vulnerable exactly when everyone is telling themselves the same story, meaning investors risk drawing the wrong conclusion from the right number.
items.angle: Classic positioning trap: if a soft print is already fully priced by the entire forecasting community, the reaction function inverts — and that's before layering in a live oil shock the consensus was built without.
items.tweetA: Every forecaster left of Bloomberg consensus is calling for a soft core CPI Tuesday. When the smart money and the dumb money agree on the same print, the trade isn't the number — it's what's already priced in. Fade the unanimous take, not the data.
items.tweetB: Soft-CPI consensus was built before Brent ripped 5% on a fresh Hormuz closure. The 'residual seasonality' story doesn't have an energy shock baked into it yet. Tuesday's print can beat expectations and still be stale by Wednesday.

charts.desc: No charts — text-only teaser, author cuts off at 'let's dig in.'

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: Banks are quietly shopping data-center debt risk transfers because buyers are 'choking' on the paper
items.idea: JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley and SMBC have been exploring private debt sales and risk transfers to reduce exposure to swelling data-center debt, per the FT. Hyperscaler leverage, bond issuance and index concentration have all surged, and progressively smaller supply waves are now producing larger spread reactions — a clear sign investor tolerance is fading even as equities remain unbothered.
items.angle: Credit market stress is preceding equity stress. The AI trade's real tell isn't the Mag7 multiple, it's whether investment-grade can clear hyperscaler paper without spread widening.
items.tweetA: Everyone's watching Nvidia's multiple. Meanwhile JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley and SMBC are quietly shopping data-center debt risk transfers because traditional buyers can't absorb the paper anymore. The AI trade's real stress test is happening in credit, not equities.
items.tweetB: Smaller bond supply waves are now producing bigger spread reactions — that's the market's way of saying position size matters more than fundamentals. If IG can't clear hyperscaler paper smoothly, the equity multiple has a funding problem, not just a growth problem.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (hyperscaler leverage / bond issuance likely charted).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: Goldman's 120-quadrillion-token forecast landed right when credit needed a distraction
items.idea: Just as data-center debt stress was surfacing in May, Goldman Sachs published 'Decoding the Agentic Economy,' projecting monthly token usage could rise toward 120 quadrillion. The timing shifted the market conversation from who is financing the AI buildout to how enormous eventual demand might become — giving credit investors a reason to keep buying hyperscaler paper instead of questioning the leverage underneath it.
items.angle: A textbook narrative rotation: a demand forecast arrived exactly when the market needed permission to stop asking about the debt.
items.tweetA: Goldman's 120 quadrillion monthly token forecast landed within days of banks quietly trying to offload data-center debt risk. Convenient timing for a narrative that needed credit investors to stop asking who's actually financing the buildout.
items.tweetB: 120 quadrillion tokens a month is the number giving credit investors permission to keep buying hyperscaler paper. Nobody's asking what price per token that requires, or whether agentic margins show up before the refinancing wall does.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (token-growth projection chart referenced in the Goldman report).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: Goldman: Hormuz bypass pipeline capacity could insulate 60%+ of Gulf exports by 2028
items.idea: Roughly 23 million b/d of pre-war exports from seven Gulf producers are tied to the Strait. Goldman's base case sees pipeline bypass capacity rising 3.8 million b/d by end-2027 and a cumulative 7.3 million b/d by end-2028 — pushing total bypass above 14 million b/d and insulating more than 60% of pre-war export volumes. Goldman raised its 3-year Brent assumption $9/bbl to $76, largely on the security premium, even as this buildout undercuts that premium over time.
items.angle: Hormuz stays a legitimate front-month trigger, but the multi-year risk premium Goldman just added to Brent has a shrinking shelf life as the bypass infrastructure gets built.
items.tweetA: Hormuz is a legitimate front-month trigger. It's a much weaker 3-year story: Goldman sees pipeline bypass capacity covering 60%+ of Gulf exports by 2028. The $9/bbl security premium they just added to the 3-year Brent forecast has a shrinking shelf life.
items.tweetB: Nine pipelines, ~2.5-year median build time, up to 7.3 million b/d of new Hormuz bypass by 2028. Trade the front-month spike, fade the back-month premium — the infrastructure fix is already under construction.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Gulf bypass-capacity buildout chart).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: SK Hynix: record FCF, trillion-dollar valuation, and institutions selling into retail leverage
items.idea: SK Hynix is forecast to generate more than $300 billion in free cash flow across 2025-2026, ahead of Micron, supporting a record $26.5 billion US share sale and entry into the trillion-dollar club. But South Korean retail investors have piled in — including via leveraged ETFs launched just as volatility rose — while global institutions sold into the rally. The company was losing money three years ago; memory remains one of tech's most cyclical businesses.
items.angle: The positioning divergence is the tell: smart money distributing into retail leverage is a classic late-cycle signature in a historically boom-bust sector.
items.tweetA: SK Hynix: record FCF, trillion-dollar valuation, and global institutions selling into Korean retail piling in via leveraged ETFs. That's the exact positioning signature that preceded the last memory bust — except retail is levered into volatility decay this time too.
items.tweetB: SK Hynix was losing money three years ago. Now it's projected to throw off $300B+ FCF in two years and everyone's calling the memory shortage structural through 2030. Every memory supercycle call in history has been followed by a glut call within 24 months.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (FCF trajectory / shareholder base charts).

items.title: Apple sues OpenAI over trade secrets — and Apple's grudges have a two-decade half-life
items.idea: Apple's lawsuit alleges ex-iPhone engineer Chang Liu exploited a leftover file-server access bug to download hardware designs after joining OpenAI's device unit, and that OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan (co-founder of io, sold to OpenAI for $6.5B alongside Jony Ive) ran interviews as information-gathering sessions. Over 400 former Apple employees have joined OpenAI's hardware division. Bloomberg notes the same dynamic playing out once before: Apple's decades-long freeze-out of Nvidia traces back to a Jobs-era dispute over Pixar IP.
items.angle: This isn't just litigation risk — it threatens the actual distribution moat (AI devices) OpenAI needs to compete beyond the API business, with a multi-year discovery process now attached.
items.tweetA: Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI reads like the Eric Schmidt/Android playbook — except this time it's not a board member leaving, it's 400+ Apple hardware engineers walking straight into OpenAI's device unit. Apple held its Nvidia grudge for two decades over a Pixar IP dispute. This one just started.
items.tweetB: OpenAI's real distribution moat isn't ChatGPT, it's the AI-device bet Jony Ive and Tang Tan are building — the same unit now facing a 40-page trade-secrets complaint. If Apple slow-walks this in discovery for years, OpenAI's hardware timeline becomes the real IPO risk, not model quality.

items.title: Iran's actual target may be $85 Brent, not chaos — and Trump 'panics' on 5-7% up moves
items.idea: MacroEdge argues Iran wants Brent around $85/bbl (with GCC nations quietly comfortable there too), and has tightened control over Hormuz because prices got 'too low' for them. Hormuz traffic is almost back to zero in mid-July, global SPRs ex-China sit near tank-bottom, and the author frames the resolution as binary: either someone escalates militarily to actually take the Strait, or the world accepts a structurally higher oil floor.
items.angle: Reframes Hormuz as a targeted price-management operation with an explicit level, not open-ended chaos — which changes how 'de-escalation' headlines should be traded.
items.tweetA: Iran's target isn't chaos, it's $85 Brent. Every de-escalation headline this month has coincided with oil trading below that level. If that's the actual reaction function, the market is trading noise while missing the price target.
items.tweetB: Global SPRs ex-China are near tank-bottom and Hormuz traffic is basically zero in mid-July. There are only two outcomes here: someone takes the Strait by force, or the world accepts a structurally higher oil floor. 'Ceasefire, oil drops 10%' isn't on that list.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Hormuz traffic chart referenced, cut off in preview).

items.title: KOSPI's sidecar circuit breaker halted over a weekend oil headline — a leverage story, not a war story
items.idea: Losses led in Asia Sunday night with the KOSPI sidecar halted at one point as Iran-war escalation hit. MacroEdge's Global Bubble Index flags South Korea specifically for extreme leverage concentrated in semiconductor and tech trades, with Japan 'not far behind.' The author expects this Asian wobble, not Hormuz itself, to drive the semiconductor/AI tape.
items.angle: The AI trade's most levered geography just tripped a circuit breaker over a weekend headline — an independent liquidity/leverage tell, separate from the oil story itself.
items.tweetA: KOSPI tripped its sidecar circuit breaker over a Sunday-night oil headline. That's not a war story, that's a leverage story — South Korea's semiconductor trade is so crowded that ordinary volatility now triggers mechanical halts. Watch Japan next.
items.tweetB: What happens when the most AI-exposed, most leveraged equity market in the world gets a geopolitical shock on a weekend, with no ability to hedge until Monday? KOSPI just showed you: a circuit-breaker halt before Asia even fully opened.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Global Bubble Index leverage chart).

items.title: Ukraine hit a refinery 800km from the front line — a second, separate supply hit to global diesel
items.idea: Ukrainian forces heavily damaged the Syzran oil refinery in Samara Oblast, roughly 800 kilometers from the frontline, triggering severe explosions as part of an intensifying long-range campaign against Russian fuel networks — while Russia simultaneously advanced on the ground near Kostyantynivka in Donetsk.
items.angle: This is a second, structurally independent hit to global refined-products supply happening at the same time as the Hormuz story — and it's being under-weighted because Hormuz is dominating the oil-supply narrative.
items.tweetA: Everyone's oil-supply story this week is Hormuz. Ukraine just hit a refinery 800km from the front line — deep-strike damage to Russian refining capacity. Two separate supply-side hits to global diesel at the same time is not being priced as two separate risks.
items.tweetB: 800 kilometers — that's how deep Ukraine's long-range strikes on Russian fuel infrastructure now reach. Add that to a Hormuz-constrained Gulf and refined products have two structurally impaired supply chains feeding the same diesel/jet-fuel shortage.

items.title: BCA's Papic: Hormuz risk is a continuum, not binary — and El Niño is about to hit food from a different angle
items.idea: Urea (fertilizer) prices have mimicked crude oil's moves this year. BCA Research's Marko Papic argues markets should stop treating Hormuz as open/closed and instead use the 1980s Tanker Wars as the framework — four years of Gulf warfare during which oil still got through. Separately, Oxford Economics flags El Niño as a bigger threat than the Gulf disruption for Southeast Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, working through domestic rice/maize supply disruption in thin-traded markets rather than a global price shock. Fertilizer prices already rose 30%+ early in the conflict.
items.angle: Two independent supply shocks — Hormuz raising fertilizer input costs, El Niño cutting crop yields — are hitting the same food system through different transmission channels at the same time, and it lands hardest on EM food importers, not the US.
items.tweetA: Papic's framework: Hormuz risk isn't 0 or 1, it's a continuum — like the 1980s Tanker Wars, four years of Gulf warfare where oil still got through. Markets pricing this as binary open/closed are using the wrong model, which is exactly why urea and Brent keep moving together.
items.tweetB: Two unrelated shocks are about to hit the same food system from different directions: Hormuz raises fertilizer input costs, El Niño cuts crop yields in Southeast Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. Different channel, same outcome — and it lands hardest where food is the biggest CPI weight, not in the US.

charts.desc: Multiple embedded charts: urea vs. crude price overlay, fertilizer benchmark index, Bloomberg Agriculture Subindex, El Niño exposure by region. CHART WORTH SAVING — the urea/Brent overlay tells the whole story in one image.

items.title: Japan is directing its $1.6 trillion pension fund to bring money home — read-through for US equity flows
items.idea: Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama announced measures pushing big Japanese investors, including the $1.6 trillion GPIF, to invest more domestically — triggering the biggest JGB yield drop since last year's Liberation Day tariff shock. The GPIF's own top-15 holdings have already shortened from 9.6-year weighted average maturity (Mar 2024) to 7.3 years (Mar 2026), itself pressuring long yields upward. Deutsche Bank's Saravelos says the yen strengthens either via faster BOJ hikes or 'a more concerted effort to shift domestic investment back onshore' — and SpectraMarkets' Brent Donnelly calls it a possible watershed: 'you know who controls the Japanese pension funds? The Japanese government.'
items.angle: This isn't the standard yen-carry-unwind trade — it's a government directly steering the world's largest pension fund's capital allocation, with direct read-through to US equity flows since the US is two-thirds of global market cap.
items.tweetA: This isn't the yen-carry-unwind story everyone's trading. Japan's Finance Ministry is directly steering the $1.6T GPIF to bring money home. When a government owns the world's largest pension fund and starts weaponizing its allocation, that's capital control by another name.
items.tweetB: US equities are two-thirds of global market cap, funded in part by exactly the kind of foreign pension flow Japan just started directing home. The UK is doing something similar. If this is the start of a broader repatriation wave, it's structurally dollar-negative regardless of what the Fed does Tuesday.

charts.desc: Multiple embedded charts: 10-yr JGB yield drop, 30-yr JGB vs. Bund spread, GPIF holdings maturity / SocGen (Edwards) yield chart. CHART WORTH SAVING — the JGB yield-drop chart.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: Goldman's Brian Garrett: call skew has nearly vanished during a live two-front war
items.idea: Call skew on the average stock has nearly disappeared, with 25-delta calls trading close to at-the-money volatility and skew at its lowest level in a decade. The VIX sits in a 14 handle and Goldman's panic indicator has collapsed to a '1 handle' — its lowest since Covid-era calm. Garrett's own framing captures the drift: March's 'Green Dot Sundays' (traders waking early to check overnight Iran-war moves) have become 'maybe Green Dot Mondays' four months later, with the same war still live.
items.angle: The single cleanest complacency read across the entire set — a live two-front war (Iran, Ukraine) and the options market is charging almost nothing to hedge against it.
items.tweetA: There's a live war in the Gulf, tankers are getting hit, and the options market is charging you almost nothing extra to hedge against it. Goldman's panic indicator just hit its lowest level since Covid-era calm. That's not confidence, that's amnesia priced into vol.
items.tweetB: March: 'Green Dot Sundays,' traders waking up before Asia opens to check if the Iran war moved markets overnight. July: same war, worse headlines, 'maybe Green Dot Mondays.' Skew is at a decade low. Complacency compounds until it doesn't.

charts.desc: Options skew chart — 25-delta call skew vs. at-the-money vol, decade-low reading. CHART WORTH SAVING, per the author's own framing ('the chart you cannot unsee').

items.title: BTC holds $63,200 while stablecoin supply quietly shrinks $10B since May
items.idea: BTC retreated from ~$64,400 to $63,400-$63,600 as Brent's spike strengthened the dollar; support sits at $63,200, then $62,100, then a $60,000 structural floor. ETH is the relative-strength leader at $1,800-$1,815; SOL remains stuck below $80; XRP sits at $1.08 ahead of possible CLARITY Act language. Fear & Greed reads 26 (Fear zone), total crypto cap is ~$2.26T, and stablecoin supply has fallen roughly $10 billion since May even as ETF inflows have only recently improved.
items.angle: The $10B stablecoin-supply decline is the more important number than any of the price levels — the liquidity pool that's supposed to buy every dip is shrinking even while price action looks orderly.
items.tweetA: BTC holding $63,200 looks like resilience. Stablecoin supply down $10B since May while price barely moved says something else: the liquidity that's supposed to buy every dip is shrinking. Orderly price action on a shrinking liquidity base isn't the same as strength. $BTC
items.tweetB: Fear & Greed at 26, BTC dominance at 56.3%, and both alts (SOL, XRP) stuck below key levels. This isn't risk-off, it's rotation-off — capital isn't leaving crypto, it's consolidating into BTC and sitting still. $ETH is the one major actually showing relative strength here.

items.title: Strategy's $216M BTC sale exposes the crack in the crypto-treasury-company trade
items.idea: Strategy sold $216 million of Bitcoin, adding to a corporate-treasury supply overhang, while several other crypto-treasury deals have failed outright — a sign investors no longer reward balance-sheet-thin 'wrapper' vehicles just for holding tokens. Valuations are shifting from token holdings to funding quality. In the same news cycle, BNY's planned USDC services show regulated stablecoin infrastructure still expanding even as speculative treasury vehicles weaken.
items.angle: The 2024-25 'buy the public wrapper, get leveraged BTC exposure' trade is quietly unwinding into forced selling at the same moment regulated institutional crypto infrastructure is scaling — a bifurcation, not a uniform risk-off signal.
items.tweetA: The crypto-treasury-company trade was 'buy the stock, get leveraged BTC exposure for free.' Strategy just sold $216M of Bitcoin and treasury-wrapper valuations are being repriced on funding quality, not token holdings. The premium-to-NAV playbook is what's breaking, not BTC itself.
items.tweetB: Same week: a public BTC treasury company is forced to sell $216M of Bitcoin, and BNY is expanding regulated USDC services. Speculative crypto wrappers are unwinding exactly as regulated crypto infrastructure scales. That's not a crypto risk-off signal, it's a crypto bifurcation signal.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: The cleaner Hormuz trade isn't long oil — it's higher yields, a firmer dollar, and shorting duration
items.idea: As oil rallied and equity futures softened on renewed strikes, gold moved the opposite direction — down — which the author reads as proof the market is trading the inflationary consequences of the conflict, not the conflict itself. Brent pushed toward $78.50, the 10-year yield toward 4.60%, and the author argues refined products (diesel, jet fuel, freight) transmit inflation to the economy faster than the Brent headline alone.
items.angle: Reframes what 'trading Hormuz' should actually mean for a portfolio — not an energy-sector long, but a cross-asset rates/FX/duration trade.
items.tweetA: Long oil is the obvious Hormuz trade and probably the wrong one. The cleaner expression is higher yields, a firmer dollar, and shorting the most expensive, longest-duration equities — because that's what actually repriced this weekend while gold, the supposed 'fear hedge,' went down.
items.tweetB: Gold fell while Iran and the US traded strikes over the weekend. That's the tell: the market isn't pricing war risk, it's pricing the inflation and rate consequences of war risk. Trade the 10-year, not the missile count.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (cross-asset performance table likely charted).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Substack)
items.title: Swaps now price 40bps of Fed tightening by December, up from 15bps in early June
items.idea: Rates swaps have moved from pricing roughly 15 basis points of tightening in early June to close to 40 basis points by December — a shift that happened while the S&P sat near record highs on AI optimism. Goldman Sachs estimates rising AI-related input costs (memory, semiconductors) could add materially to core PCE by year-end, and Kevin Warsh's preference for less forward guidance in his first congressional testimony as Fed Chair means a wider distribution of rate outcomes.
items.angle: The rates market has quietly flipped from pricing cuts to pricing hikes in six weeks, hidden beneath the AI-capex bull narrative rather than caused by a separate story — the bull case for AI stocks and the hawkish case for the Fed are converging.
items.tweetA: 40bps of Fed tightening now priced for December, up from 15bps in early June. That move happened while the S&P sat near record highs and everyone talked about AI capex. The rates market already flipped from cuts to hikes — equities haven't gotten the memo yet.
items.tweetB: AI capex was supposed to be deflationary long-run, inflationary short-run. Goldman's now pricing rising memory and semiconductor costs directly into core PCE. The bull case for AI stocks and the hawkish case for the Fed are increasingly the same chart.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Fed-pricing swaps curve likely charted).
